statement: AM-GM inequality: for positive reals $x, y, x + y \geq 2\sqrt{x y}$, equivalently $\sqrt{x y} \leq (x+y)/2$
Cauchy-Schwarz in the form: $(\sum_i u_i v_i)^2 \leq (\sum_i u_i^2)(\sum_i v_i^2)$

For positive real numbers $a$, $b$, $c$ satisfying $(a+b-c)\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}-\frac{1}{c}\right)=3$, we have
\[
\frac{a+b}{c}+c\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)\ge 2+2\sqrt{3}.
\]
Equality holds if and only if $ab=c^2$ and $\frac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt{3}$.
proof:
Define $p=\frac{a+b}{c}$ and $q=c\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)$. Then
\[
(a+b-c)\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}-\frac{1}{c}\right)=c(p-1)\cdot\frac{q-1}{c}=(p-1)(q-1)=3.
\]
Assume for contradiction that $p-1$ and $q-1$ are both negative. Then $a+b<c$ and $\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{c}$; multiplying gives $(a+b)\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)<1$. But by AM–GM,
\[
(a+b)\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)=2+\frac{a}{b}+\frac{b}{a}\ge 2+2\sqrt{\frac{a}{b}\cdot\frac{b}{a}}=4,
\]
a contradiction. Hence $p-1$ and $q-1$ are both positive, i.e. $p>1$, $q>1$.

Apply AM–GM to the positive numbers $p-1$ and $q-1$:
\[
(p-1)+(q-1)\ge 2\sqrt{(p-1)(q-1)}=2\sqrt{3},
\]
so $p+q\ge 2+2\sqrt{3}$, which is the desired inequality.

Equality in AM–GM occurs iff $p-1=q-1$, i.e. $p=q$, and then $(p-1)^2=3$ gives $p=q=1+\sqrt{3}$. The condition $p=q$ simplifies to $\frac{a+b}{c}=c\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)$, which is equivalent to $ab=c^2$. Therefore equality holds iff $ab=c^2$ and $\frac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt{3}$.

statement:
For positive real numbers $a$, $b$, $c$ satisfying $(a+b-c)\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}-\frac{1}{c}\right)=3$, we have
\[
(a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2})\left(\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}-\frac{1}{c^{2}}\right)\ge 13+4\sqrt{3},
\]
with equality if and only if $ab=c^{2}$ and $\frac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt{3}$.
proof:
Set $p=\frac{a+b}{c}$ and $q=c\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)$. From the given condition we obtain $(p-1)(q-1)=3$, and the earlier argument shows $p>1$, $q>1$. Using $ab=c^{2}\,p/q$ (derived from the definitions of $p$ and $q$), compute
\begin{align*}
a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2}&=c^{2}\!\left(p^{2}-\frac{2p}{q}-1\right),\\[2mm]
\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}-\frac{1}{c^{2}}&=\frac{1}{c^{2}}\!\left(q^{2}-\frac{2q}{p}-1\right).
\end{align*}
Hence
\[
R:=(a^{2}+b^{2}-c^{2})\left(\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}-\frac{1}{c^{2}}\right)
   =\left(p^{2}-\frac{2p}{q}-1\right)\!\left(q^{2}-\frac{2q}{p}-1\right).
\]

From $(p-1)(q-1)=3$ we have $pq=p+q+2$. Eliminating $q$ and simplifying yields a rational function of $p$ alone:
\[
R(p)=\frac{4p^{4}+5p^{3}+13p^{2}+6p+8}{p\,(p^{2}+p-2)},\qquad p>1.
\]

Differentiating $R(p)$ gives $R'(p)=0$ only at $p=1+\sqrt{3}$ (among $p>1$). Since $R(p)\to\infty$ as $p\to1^{+}$ and as $p\to\infty$, this critical point is the global minimum. Substituting $p=1+\sqrt{3}$ yields $R_{\min}=13+4\sqrt{3}$.

At the minimum we have $p=1+\sqrt{3}$, and the relation $(p-1)(q-1)=3$ forces $q=p$. The equality $p=q$ is equivalent to $\frac{a+b}{c}=c\bigl(\frac1a+\frac1b\bigr)$, which simplifies to $ab=c^{2}$. Therefore equality holds precisely when $ab=c^{2}$ and $\frac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt{3}$.

statement:
For positive real numbers \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) satisfying \((a+b-c)\bigl(\frac1a+\frac1b-\frac1c\bigr)=3\), we have
\[
\bigl(a^{4}+b^{4}-c^{4}\bigr)\Bigl(\frac1{a^{4}}+\frac1{b^{4}}-\frac1{c^{4}}\Bigr)\ge 361+208\sqrt3,
\]
with equality if and only if \(ab=c^{2}\) and \(\dfrac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt3\).
proof:
Set \(x=\frac{a}{c}\), \(y=\frac{b}{c}\). The given condition becomes \((x+y-1)\bigl(\frac1x+\frac1y-1\bigr)=3\). Let \(s=x+y\) and \(t=xy\) (so \(s>0\), \(t>0\)). From the condition,
\[
(s-1)\Bigl(\frac{s}{t}-1\Bigr)=3\;\Longrightarrow\;t=\frac{s(s-1)}{s+2},\qquad s>1.
\]

Now
\[
x^{4}+y^{4}=(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}-2x^{2}y^{2}=((s^{2}-2t)^{2}-2t^{2}),
\qquad
\frac1{x^{4}}+\frac1{y^{4}}=\frac{x^{4}+y^{4}}{t^{4}}.
\]
Hence
\[
F(s):=\bigl(x^{4}+y^{4}-1\bigr)\Bigl(\frac1{x^{4}}+\frac1{y^{4}}-1\Bigr)
      =\Bigl((s^{2}-2t)^{2}-2t^{2}-1\Bigr)
        \Bigl(\frac{(s^{2}-2t)^{2}-2t^{2}}{t^{4}}-1\Bigr).
\]

Substituting \(t=\frac{s(s-1)}{s+2}\) and simplifying yields the rational function
\[
F(s)=\frac{8s^{10}+8s^{9}+40s^{8}+97s^{7}+161s^{6}+251s^{5}+383s^{4}+456s^{3}+348s^{2}+160s+32}
      {s^{2}\bigl(s^{5}+s^{4}-5s^{3}-s^{2}+8s-4\bigr)},\qquad s>1.
\]

Differentiating \(F(s)\) gives
\[
F'(s)=\frac{8(3s^{12}+6s^{11}-8s^{10}-4s^{9}-68s^{8}-184s^{7}-336s^{6}-512s^{5}-580s^{4}-408s^{3}-128s^{2}+16s+16)}
          {s^{3}(s^{7}+2s^{6}-6s^{5}-8s^{4}+17s^{3}+6s^{2}-20s+8)}.
\]
The numerator factors as \((s^{2}+2)(s^{2}-2s-2)(s^{4}+2s^{2}+4s+2)(3s^{4}+12s^{3}+10s^{2}+4s-2)\), so the only real root of \(F'(s)=0\) in \(s>1\) is \(s=1+\sqrt3\) (the root of \(s^{2}-2s-2=0\)). Since \(\lim\limits_{s\to1^{+}}F(s)=+\infty\) and \(\lim\limits_{s\to\infty}F(s)=+\infty\), this critical point is the global minimum. Evaluating,
\[
F(1+\sqrt3)=361+208\sqrt3.
\]

Returning to the original variables, \(s=\frac{a+b}{c}\) and \(t=xy=\frac{ab}{c^{2}}\). At the minimum we have \(s=1+\sqrt3\); then from \(t=\frac{s(s-1)}{s+2}\) we obtain \(t=1\), i.e. \(ab=c^{2}\). Thus equality holds precisely when \(ab=c^{2}\) and \(\frac{a+b}{c}=1+\sqrt3\).
